steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to filter /var/log/app/access.log down to only requests that hit our internal API endpoints — anything matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` — and dump those lines to /home/user/internal_hits.log. But here's the thing: we're also seeing a ton of health check spam from the load balancer that I want excluded, those come from 10.0.0.0/8 range and hit `/health` or `/ready`. So internal API calls yes, but not if they're from the 10.x.x.x health probes.

Also the timestamps in that log are in that annoying Apache combined format with the brackets, would be nice if the output had them as ISO 8601 instead (like 2024-01-15T14:30:22). Rest of the line can stay as-is.

Log's about 50k lines so nothing crazy but I don't want to sit here doing it by hand.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@6b830a3b6c15:~$

Analysis: Starting fresh - I need to examine the access log file to understand its format, then create a filtering and transformation pipeline. The task requires: 1) Filter for `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` endpoints, 2) Exclude requests from 10.0.0.0/8 range to `/health` or `/ready`, 3) Convert Apache combined format timestamps to ISO 8601, 4) Output to /home/user/internal_hits.log
Plan: First, I'll check if the log file exists and examine a few sample lines to understand the exact format. Then I'll craft an appropriate grep/awk/sed pipeline to filter and transform the data.

steps.message:
Analysis: The log file exists (5.7M, ~50k lines) and uses Apache combined format. The format is: `IP - - [timestamp] "METHOD PATH HTTP/VERSION" STATUS SIZE "-" "USER_AGENT"`. I can see health checks from 10.x.x.x IPs hitting /ready. Now I need to check for lines matching the internal API pattern to understand what I'm filtering for.
Plan: I'll search for lines matching the internal API pattern to see examples, then create a script to: 1) Filter for /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ endpoints, 2) Exclude 10.x.x.x sources hitting /health or /ready, 3) Convert timestamps from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +0000] to ISO 8601 format using awk/sed.

198.170.22.163 - - [04/Apr/2024:08:16:59 +0000] "PATCH /api/v1/internal/status HTTP/1.1" 404 8876 "-" "PostmanRuntime/7.29.0"

user@6b830a3b6c15:~$

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! I found internal API endpoints. The log has entries matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` pattern (v1, v2, etc.). Now I need to create a filtering script that: 1) Includes lines with /api/v[0-9]+/internal/, 2) Excludes lines where IP starts with '10.' AND path is /health or /ready, 3) Converts timestamps from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +0000] to ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS).
Plan: I'll create an awk script that processes each line: checks if it matches the internal API pattern, excludes 10.x health checks, and converts the timestamp. The month conversion needs a lookup table (Jan->01, Feb->02, etc.). I'll write and execute this awk script to generate the filtered output.

arguments.keystrokes:
awk '
/\/api\/v[0-9]+\/internal\// {
  # Check if this is a 10.x.x.x health check to exclude
  if ($1 ~ /^10\./ && ($7 ~ /\/(health|ready)/)) {
    next
  }
  
  # Extract and convert timestamp
  match($0, /\[([0-9]{2})\/([A-Za-z]{3})\/([0-9]{4}):([0-9]{2}):([0-9]{2}):([0-9]{2})/, ts)
  
  # Month conversion
  months["Jan"]="01"; months["Feb"]="02"; months["Mar"]="03"; months["Apr"]="04"
  months["May"]="05"; months["Jun"]="06"; months["Jul"]="07"; months["Aug"]="08"
  months["Sep"]="09"; months["Oct"]="10"; months["Nov"]="11"; months["Dec"]="12"
  
  iso_timestamp = ts[3] "-" months[ts[2]] "-" ts[1] "T" ts[4]":" ts[5]":" ts[6]
  
  # Replace timestamp in original line
  sub(/\[[^\]]+\]/, iso_timestamp)
  print
}
' /var/log/app/access.log > /home/user/internal_hits.log

Plan: I'll check if the output file exists, see how many lines were filtered, and examine a few sample lines to verify the filtering and timestamp conversion worked correctly.

steps.message:
Analysis: Great! The output file was created successfully with 5,000 lines (filtered from ~50k). The timestamps are correctly converted to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS). I can see internal API endpoints (/api/v1/internal/, /api/v2/internal/, /api/v3/internal/). However, I notice line 5 shows a 10.239.32.97 IP hitting /api/v3/internal/webhook - this is correct because it's NOT a health check (it's an internal API call), so it should be included. Let me verify that health checks from 10.x IPs are actually excluded.
Plan: I'll verify the filtering worked correctly by: 1) Checking if any 10.x.x.x IPs with /health or /ready endpoints made it through (there should be none), 2) Confirming that 10.x.x.x IPs hitting actual internal API endpoints ARE included (as intended), 3) Verify all lines contain the internal API pattern.

Analysis: Perfect! The filtering is working correctly: 1) Zero lines with 10.x.x.x IPs hitting /health or /ready endpoints (health checks excluded), 2) 10.x.x.x IPs hitting actual internal API endpoints ARE included (as intended), 3) All lines contain /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ patterns, 4) Timestamps are properly converted to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS), 5) The rest of the log line format is preserved. The task has been completed successfully with 5,000 filtered lines in the output file.
Plan: Perform a final verification to confirm all lines match the internal API pattern and provide a summary of the task completion.

All requirements have been met: 1) Only lines matching /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ are included, 2) Health checks from 10.x.x.x IPs to /health or /ready are excluded (verified 0 matches), 3) Timestamps are converted from Apache format [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +0000] to ISO 8601 format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS, 4) The rest of each log line is preserved intact.
Plan: The task is complete and ready for grading. All filtering, exclusion, and transformation requirements have been successfully implemented and verified.
